sentences.grammarNote:
**よう** (yō) = casual 'hey!' greeting (primarily male casual speech). More common polite greeting: **こんにちは** (hello) or **やあ** (yā, hi).
**〜です** = polite copula 'am/is'. The subject **私は** is dropped — in Japanese, the subject is omitted when clear from context, especially in self-introductions.
Alternative self-intro forms: **〜と申します** (formal/humble) or **〜といいます** (neutral).

sentences.target_language: これは私のウェブサイトです
sentences.romanized: kore wa watashi no webusaito desu
sentences.grammarNote:
**これ** (kore) = 'this' (near the speaker, stands alone as a pronoun). Before a noun: **この** + noun: **このウェブサイト** (this website).
**ウェブサイト** (webusaito) = website — a katakana loanword. Modern tech terms are almost always in katakana.
**私の** = 'my': **の** is the possessive particle, linking the owner to the possessed: **私のウェブサイト** = 'my website'.

sentences.base_language: I like learning languages but it is very hard
sentences.target_language: 言語を学ぶのが好きですが、とても難しいです
sentences.romanized: gengo wo manabu no ga suki desu ga, totemo muzukashii desu
sentences.literal: Languages [obj] learn [nominalizer] [subj] like is but, very difficult is.
sentences.grammarNote: の (no) nominalizes the verb phrase: 学ぶの = 'the act of learning'. This turns the verb clause into a noun so it can take が as a subject marker.

sentences.base_language: I am not very good at speaking spanish
sentences.target_language: スペイン語を話すのがあまり上手ではありません
sentences.romanized: supeingo wo hanasu no ga amari jouzu dewa arimasen
sentences.grammarNote:
**あまり〜ない** (amari ~ nai) = 'not very / not particularly': **あまり** must be paired with a negative form. **あまり上手ではありません** = 'not very skilled'.
**上手ではありません** = polite negative of **上手です**: **ではありません** is the formal negative of **です**. Casual: **上手じゃない**.
**〜のが** + adjective: **の** nominalizes the verb phrase, allowing an adjective (**上手**) to describe the activity.

sentences.base_language: I learned with other apps and they were great too
sentences.target_language: 他のアプリで勉強しましたが、それも良かったです
sentences.romanized: hoka no apuri de benkyou shimashita ga, sore mo yokatta desu
sentences.literal: Other apps [location] study did but, those also good-were.
sentences.grammarNote:
**他の** (hoka no) = 'other' as a modifier: **他** (hoka, other/another) + **の** (linking to noun): **他のアプリ** = 'other apps'.
**で** marks the means/tool: **アプリで勉強する** = 'study using an app'.
**良かったです** = 'was good' (polite past of **いい**). The irregular past: **いい / 良い** → **良かった** (yōkatta).

sentences.base_language: But I made this app to do more "immersion"
sentences.target_language: でも、もっと「没入学習」をするためにこのアプリを作りました
sentences.romanized: demo, motto "botsunyuu gakushuu" wo suru tame ni kono apuri wo tsukurimashita
sentences.literal: But, more "immersion learning" [obj] do in-order-to this app [obj] made.
sentences.grammarNote: ために (tame ni) expresses purpose: 'in order to do...'. It follows a verb in plain form: するために = 'in order to do'.

**学べます** (manabemasu) = 'can learn' (polite potential form): **学ぶ** (manabu, to learn/study) → potential **学べる** → polite **学べます**.
**と一緒に** (to issho ni) = 'together with [someone]': **と** (companion particle) + **一緒に** (issho ni, together).
**学ぶ** (manabu) = to learn/study deeply (often used for more formal or serious learning, vs **勉強する** for studying schoolwork).

sentences.base_language: I tried to make this easy to use but I may have made mistakes
sentences.target_language: 使いやすくしようとしましたが、間違いがあるかもしれません
sentences.romanized: tsukaiyasuku shiyou to shimashita ga, machigai ga aru kamoshiremasen
sentences.literal: Easy-to-use make-try did but, mistakes [subj] exist maybe.
sentences.grammarNote:
**使いやすい** (tsukaieasui) = 'easy to use': **使う** (tsukau, use) + **やすい** (yasui, easy to do). The **〜やすい** suffix attaches to a verb stem to mean 'easy to [verb]': **食べやすい** (easy to eat), **読みやすい** (easy to read).
**〜ようとする** = 'try to do': **しよう** (volitional) + **と** (quotation/intention) + **する** (do).
**かもしれません** = 'might/may': polite form of **かもしれない** (kamo shirenai).

sentences.base_language: If you want to help me, that would be cool
sentences.target_language: 手伝ってくれたら嬉しいです
sentences.grammarNote: くれたら (kuretara) is the conditional of くれる (kureru), a giving verb used when someone does something for the speaker. てくれたら = 'if you do [it] for me'.

sentences.base_language: If you like this website, please share it
sentences.target_language: このウェブサイトが気に入ったら、シェアしてください
sentences.romanized: kono webusaito ga ki ni ittara, shea shite kudasai
sentences.grammarNote:
**気に入る** (ki ni iru) = 'to like/take a liking to': **気** (ki, spirit/feeling) + **に入る** (ni hairu/iru, enter into). **気に入ったら** = 'if/when you like it'.
**シェアする** (shea suru) = 'to share' — a modern loanword. **シェアしてください** = 'please share'.
**〜てください** = 'please do ~': te-form + **ください** is the standard polite request for actions.

sentences.base_language: If you know other languages, you can make lessons!
sentences.target_language: 他の言語ができたら、レッスンを作れます！
sentences.romanized: hoka no gengo ga dekitara, ressun wo tsukuremasu!
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ができる** (ga dekiru) = 'can do / be capable of': **言語ができる** = 'can [use] the language'. This pattern expresses general ability with a subject.
**できたら** = conditional 'if [you] can': **できる** → past/potential **できた** + conditional **ら**.
**作れます** = 'can make' (potential polite): **作る** (tsukuru) → potential **作れる** → polite **作れます**.

sentences.base_language: If you can't help that is OK
sentences.target_language: 手伝えなくても大丈夫です
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜なくても** = 'even if [you] don't/can't ~': **〜ない** (negative) + **くても** (even if). **手伝えなくても** = 'even if you can't help'.
**手伝えない** = 'cannot help' (negative potential): **手伝う** (tetsudau, to help) → potential **手伝える** → negative **手伝えない**.
**大丈夫** (daijōbu) = 'okay/fine/no problem': **大** (dai, big) + **丈夫** (jōbu, sturdy/healthy).

sentences.base_language: I am happy you are learning with me either way
sentences.target_language: どちらにしても、一緒に学んでくれて嬉しいです
sentences.romanized: dochira ni shitemo, issho ni manande kurete ureshii desu
sentences.literal: Either-way, together learn give-and happy is.
sentences.grammarNote:
**どちらにしても** = 'either way / regardless': **どちら** (which of two) + **にしても** (even if it is). A flexible phrase for 'in any case'.
**学んでくれて** = 'that you learned for me' (grateful nuance): **くれる** (kureru) marks that the action benefits the speaker. **〜てくれて嬉しい** = 'I'm happy that you did [it] for me'.
**嬉しい** (ureshii) = happy/glad; an い-adjective expressing personal emotional happiness.
